column1: #This mapping was created following and expanding upon the SSSOM guidance: https://mapping-commons.github.io/sssom/spec/

column3: ext_subject_definition
column12: ext_object_definition
column24: comment

column3: BOLD System generated unique identifier for the sample being sequenced.
User generated identifier for the sample being sequenced, often identical to the Field ID or Museum ID.
column12: A local identifier or name that for the material sample used for extracting nucleic acids, and subsequent sequencing. It can refer either to the original material collected or to any derived sub-samples. It can have any format, but we suggest that you make it concise, unique and consistent within your lab, and as informative as possible. INSDC requires every sample name from a single Submitter to be unique. Use of a globally unique identifier for the field source_mat_id is recommended in addition to sample_name
column24: Both terms have a close meaning, however the field description for the object (MIxS) details more specific recommendations 'concise, unique and consistent within your lab, and as informative as possible. INSDC requires every sample name from a single Submitter to be unique.'. Whereas, the subject, details that the identifier can be identical to Field or Museum ID.

column3: Specimen or sample identifier generated in the field or lot number for a bulk collection event.
column12: A local identifier or name that for the material sample used for extracting nucleic acids, and subsequent sequencing. It can refer either to the original material collected or to any derived sub-samples. It can have any format, but we suggest that you make it concise, unique and consistent within your lab, and as informative as possible. INSDC requires every sample name from a single Submitter to be unique. Use of a globally unique identifier for the field source_mat_id is recommended in addition to sample_name
column24: These terms are a close match when the subject 'fieldid' is used for initial portion of the description 'Specimen or sample identifier generated in the field'. If the subject is used as the latter part of the description 'lot number for a bulk collection event.', the subject would be a closer match to 'source_mat_id' for the object.

column3: Specimen or sample identifier generated in the field or lot number for a bulk collection event.
column12: A unique identifier assigned to a material sample (as defined by http://rs.tdwg.org/dwc/terms/materialSampleID, and as opposed to a particular digital record of a material sample) used for extracting nucleic acids, and subsequent sequencing. The identifier can refer either to the original material collected or to any derived sub-samples. The INSDC qualifiers /specimen_voucher, /bio_material, or /culture_collection may or may not share the same value as the source_mat_id field. For instance, the /specimen_voucher qualifier and source_mat_id may both contain 'UAM:Herps:14' , referring to both the specimen voucher and sampled tissue with the same identifier. However, the /culture_collection qualifier may refer to a value from an initial culture (e.g. ATCC:11775) while source_mat_id would refer to an identifier from some derived culture from which the nucleic acids were extracted (e.g. xatc123 or ark:/2154/R2)
column24: These terms are a close match when the subject 'fieldid' is used for the latter part of the description 'lot number for a bulk collection event.'. If the subject is used for the initial portion of the description 'Specimen or sample identifier generated in the field' it would be a closer match to 'samp_name' for the object.

column3: Catalog number from a museum collection.
column12: A unique identifier assigned to a material sample (as defined by http://rs.tdwg.org/dwc/terms/materialSampleID, and as opposed to a particular digital record of a material sample) used for extracting nucleic acids, and subsequent sequencing. The identifier can refer either to the original material collected or to any derived sub-samples. The INSDC qualifiers /specimen_voucher, /bio_material, or /culture_collection may or may not share the same value as the source_mat_id field. For instance, the /specimen_voucher qualifier and source_mat_id may both contain 'UAM:Herps:14' , referring to both the specimen voucher and sampled tissue with the same identifier. However, the /culture_collection qualifier may refer to a value from an initial culture (e.g. ATCC:11775) while source_mat_id would refer to an identifier from some derived culture from which the nucleic acids were extracted (e.g. xatc123 or ark:/2154/R2)
column24: The subject can be related to source material identifiers as it can describe the original material collected that will derive sub-sample but it is less similar to the object than fieldid.

column3: A BOLD generated identifier for the marker and specimen sequence combination.
BOLD System generated numerical ID associated with the specimen record.

column24: Semantically, these terms may have a somewhat related meaning but the expected syntax may be different.

column3: Code associated with a given collection within an institution.
column12: A unique identifier assigned to a material sample (as defined by http://rs.tdwg.org/dwc/terms/materialSampleID, and as opposed to a particular digital record of a material sample) used for extracting nucleic acids, and subsequent sequencing. The identifier can refer either to the original material collected or to any derived sub-samples. The INSDC qualifiers /specimen_voucher, /bio_material, or /culture_collection may or may not share the same value as the source_mat_id field. For instance, the /specimen_voucher qualifier and source_mat_id may both contain 'UAM:Herps:14' , referring to both the specimen voucher and sampled tissue with the same identifier. However, the /culture_collection qualifier may refer to a value from an initial culture (e.g. ATCC:11775) while source_mat_id would refer to an identifier from some derived culture from which the nucleic acids were extracted (e.g. xatc123 or ark:/2154/R2)
column24: Semantically, these terms may have a somewhat related meaning but the expected syntax may be different.

column3: Institution storing the physical specimen.
column12: The name of the institution where the sample or DNA extract is held or "sample not available" if the sample was used in its entirety for analysis or otherwise not retained
column24: Both bubject and object expect the name of the institution storing the specimen, as such, semantically, both are exact matches. However, syntactically, if the sample is not available, the object expects string 'sample not available', wheras the subject details no requirement.

column3: Identifier associated with the taxon assignment.
column12: NCBI taxon id of the sample. Maybe be a single taxon or mixed taxa sample. Use 'synthetic metagenome for mock community/positive controls, or 'blank sample' for negative controls
column24: Both bubject and object expect the taxonomy identifier for the specimen. However, the object requires it to be detailed as a NCBI ID, whereas the subject expects the ID according to BOLD taxonomic backbone. However, syntactically, if the sample is not available, the object expects string 'sample not available', wheras the subject details no requirement. Syntactically, there is no association as the values will not be related as they belong to two different systems. Syntactic normalization could be possible with a mapping between differnet taxonomic systems, but, even then, the subject would still have a skos:narrowMatch with the object, as MIxS expects both the taxonomic ID and written nomenclature.

Taxonomy: Phylum.

Reference for species name/Authorship of the species name.
Taxonomic identification of the specimen.
column12: NCBI taxon id of the sample. Maybe be a single taxon or mixed taxa sample. Use 'synthetic metagenome for mock community/positive controls, or 'blank sample' for negative controls
column24: Both terms expect the taxonomic identification of the specimen. However, the object expect both the numerical ID and the textual nomenclature, as such, the subject is narrower, as it expect only the textual nomecalture, semantically and syntactically.

column3: The method used to identify the specimen.
column12: Method used for taxonomic classification, along with reference database used, classification rank, and thresholds used to classify new genomes
column24: Both terms expect the method used for taxonomic classification of the specimien. However, the object contains not only the method, but also, reference databases used, classification rank and thresholds; as such, the subject is narrower, as it expect only the method, semantically and syntactically.

column3: Taxonomic rank of the identification associated with the specimen.
column12: Method used for taxonomic classification, along with reference database used, classification rank, and thresholds used to classify new genomes
column24: Both terms expect the taxonomy rank of the specimen identification. However, the object contains not only the rank, but also, reference databases used, method and thresholds; as such, the subject is narrower, as it expect only the rank, semantically and syntactically.

column3: Full name of primary individual responsible for providing the taxonomic identification of the specimen.
Identifier email address.
General notes regarding the taxonomy of the specimen.
The sex of the specimen registered in the record. This field uses a controlled vocabulary: 'Male', 'female', 'hermaphrodite' only.
column12: Physical sex of the host
column24: Both terms expect the sex of the specimen. However, the object expect the sex for a particular organism of the urobiome, as such, the subject is broader semantically but related syntatically, as the object requires strings from a controlled vocabulary.

column3: The sex of the specimen registered in the record. This field uses a controlled vocabulary: 'Male', 'female', 'hermaphrodite' only.
column12: The sex and reproductive status of the food animal
column24: Both terms expect the sex of the specimen. However, the object expect the sex for a particular organism of the animal class, as such, the subject is broader semantically but related syntatically, as the object requires strings from a controlled vocabulary.

column3: The sex of the specimen registered in the record. This field uses a controlled vocabulary: 'Male', 'female', 'hermaphrodite' only.
column12: Gender or physical sex of the host
column24: Both terms expect the sex of the specimen. However, the object expect the sex for a particular organism from the host, as such, the subject is broader semantically but related syntatically.

column3: The sex of the specimen registered in the record. This field uses a controlled vocabulary: 'Male', 'female', 'hermaphrodite' only.
column12: Sex of the reproductive parts on the whole plant, e.g. pistillate, staminate, monoecieous, hermaphrodite
column24: Both terms expect the sex of the specimen. However, the object expect the sex for a particular organism from the plant class, as such, the subject is broader semantically but related syntatically, as the object requires strings from a controlled vocabulary.

column3: Sexual/asexual/cyclic parthenogen only.
column12: The type of reproduction from the parent stock. Values for this field is specific to different taxa. For phage or virus: lytic/lysogenic/temperate/obligately lytic. For plasmids: incompatibility group. For eukaryotes: sexual/asexual
column14: Expected_value: for virus: lytic, lysogenic, temperate, obligately lytic; for plasmid: incompatibility group; for eukaryote: asexual, sexual; other more specific values (e.g., incompatibility group) are allowed
column24: Semantically, both terms expect the type of reproduction, however the subject specifies 'Sexual/asexual/cyclic parthenogen only'. Syntatically, the object recommends values for the field 'Expected_value: for virus: lytic, lysogenic, temperate, obligately lytic; for plasmid: incompatibility group; for eukaryote: asexual, sexual; other more specific values (e.g., incompatibility group) are allowed'.

column3: The age class or life stage of the specimen(s) at the time of sampling. Contolled vocabulary: 'Adult', 'Immature', 'Juvenile', 'Pupa'.
column12: Description of life stage of host
column24: Both terms expect the life stage, however, the object expects it only for the host, as such, the subject is broader as it does not do that distinction. Syntatically, the object expects only a 'stage', whereas the subject expects a controlled vocabularry ''Adult', 'Immature', 'Juvenile', 'Pupa'.'.

column3: A brief note or term associated with the specimen to add context to the record.
Detailed notes regarding the specimen.

Status of the specimen in an accessioning process.This field uses a controlled vocabulary: 'Museum Vouchered:Type', 'Museum Vouchered:Type Series', 'Vouchered:Registered Collection', 'To Be Vouchered:Holdup/Private', 'E-Vouchered:DNA/Tissue+Photo', 'Dna/Tissue Vouchered Only', 'No Specimen'. For type series, use of GBIF's typeStatus vocabulary(https://rs.gbif.org/vocabulary/gbif/type_status.xml) is required.
A physical result of a sampling (or subsampling) event. In biological collections, the material sample is typically collected, and either preserved or destructively processed.
Link to an external resource for the specimen record.
column12: A related resource that is referenced, cited, or otherwise associated to the sequence
column24: Both terms expect a reference to an external resources related to the specimen. Syntatically, the object expects a structured pattern ('^({PMID}|{DOI}|{URL})$'), where the subject makes no recommendations.

column3: A list (concatenated and comma separated) of other specimens associated with the subject specimen at the time of its collection. References to other specimen identifiers should be preceded by the relationship. Examples: 'host: PLANT23452, prey: USNM45677' when both prey and host specimens have been captured.

A list (concatenated and comma separated) of taxa associated with the specimen at the time of its collection. References to taxa should be preceded by the relationship. Use of this field implies knowledge of an associated specimen where the identification is an inference. Examples: [host: Quercus alba], [prey: caterpillar].
Comma separated list of full or abbreviated names of the individuals or teams responsible for collecting the sample in the field.
The start date for the associated collecting event.
column12: The time of sampling, either as an instance (single point in time) or interval. In case no exact time is available, the date/time can be right truncated i.e. all of these are valid times: 2008-01-23T19:23:10+00:00; 2008-01-23T19:23:10; 2008-01-23; 2008-01; 2008; Except: 2008-01; 2008 all are ISO8601 compliant
column24: The object expects the date and time of the sampling, either as a single point or as an interval; whereas, the subject specifically expects the start date of the collection, meaning it expects a narrower value. Syntatically, these may be an exact match or a narrow match depending on how the user represents the value.

column3: The end date for the associated collecting event.
column12: The time of sampling, either as an instance (single point in time) or interval. In case no exact time is available, the date/time can be right truncated i.e. all of these are valid times: 2008-01-23T19:23:10+00:00; 2008-01-23T19:23:10; 2008-01-23; 2008-01; 2008; Except: 2008-01; 2008 all are ISO8601 compliant
column24: The object expects the date and time of the sampling, either as a single point or as an interval; whereas, the subject specifically expects the end date of the collection, meaning it expects a narrower value. Syntatically, these may be an exact match or a narrow match depending on how the user represents the value.

column3: An identifier for the set of information associated with an Event (something that occurs at a place and time). May be a global unique identifier or an identifier specific to the data set.
The time or time of day during which the sample was collected.
column12: The time of sampling, either as an instance (single point in time) or interval. In case no exact time is available, the date/time can be right truncated i.e. all of these are valid times: 2008-01-23T19:23:10+00:00; 2008-01-23T19:23:10; 2008-01-23; 2008-01; 2008; Except: 2008-01; 2008 all are ISO8601 compliant
column24: The object expects the date and time of the sampling, either as a single point or as an interval; whereas, the subject specifically expects the time of the collection, meaning it expects a narrower value. Syntatically, these may be an exact match or a narrow match depending on how the user represents the value.

column3: Comments or notes about the collection event.
Unique identifier assigned to the most detailed or specific geographic division available, either country/ocean or province/state.
The full, unabbreviated name of the country, major political unit, or ocean.
column12: The geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name. Country or sea names should be chosen from the INSDC country list (http://insdc.org/country.html), or the GAZ ontology (http://purl.bioontology.org/ontology/GAZ)
column24: The object expects the geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name, whereas the subject expects only the country or ocean. The object expects a structured pattern ('^{country}: {region}, {specific_location}$'), where country/ocean would be the first string delimited by a semicolon, whereas the object would expect only the country/ocean name, so it is narrower.

column3: Country ISO 3166-1 Alpha-2 code.
The full, unabbreviated name of the state, province, territory, or prefecture within the given country.
column12: The geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name. Country or sea names should be chosen from the INSDC country list (http://insdc.org/country.html), or the GAZ ontology (http://purl.bioontology.org/ontology/GAZ)
column24: The object expects the geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name, whereas the subject expects only the providence or state. The object expects a structured pattern ('^{country}: {region}, {specific_location}$'), where region would be the second string delimited by a space, whereas the object would expect only the providence/state, so it is narrower. Here region from the object could directly or indirectly point to providence/state as a region.

column3: Park, county, district, lake or river.
column12: The geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name. Country or sea names should be chosen from the INSDC country list (http://insdc.org/country.html), or the GAZ ontology (http://purl.bioontology.org/ontology/GAZ)
column24: The object expects the geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name, whereas the subject expects only the region name. The object expects a structured pattern ('^{country}: {region}, {specific_location}$'), where region would be the second string delimited by a space, whereas the object would expect only the region name, so it is narrower.

column3: Sector of park or county/city.
The name of the sampling location.
column12: The geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name. Country or sea names should be chosen from the INSDC country list (http://insdc.org/country.html), or the GAZ ontology (http://purl.bioontology.org/ontology/GAZ)
column24: The object expects the geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name, whereas the subject expects only the specific site name. The object expects a structured pattern ('^{country}: {region}, {specific_location}$'), where specific location would be the last string delimited by a comma, whereas the object would expect only the specific site name, so it is narrower.

column3: A short string representing the sampling location.
Latitude & Longitude in decimal degrees (DD) format (e.g. 45.837).
column12: The geographical origin of the sample as defined by latitude and longitude. The values should be reported in decimal degrees, limited to 8 decimal points, and in WGS84 system
column24: Both terms expect the latitude and longitude in degrees format. Syntatically, the object requires a structured pattern ('^{lat} {lon}$') that seems to align with the subject, however the subject provides no clear example for the strucutre, although its expected format is an array.

column3: Accuracy of coordinate in decimal degrees (DD).
The source of the coordinates.
Elevation of sampling site. Measured in meters relative to sea level. Negative values indicate a position below sea level.
column12: Elevation of the sampling site is its height above a fixed reference point, most commonly the mean sea level. Elevation is mainly used when referring to points on the earth's surface, while altitude is used for points above the surface, such as an aircraft in flight or a spacecraft in orbit
column24: Both terms have the same meaning, although the object has some ambiguity, allowing for a reference point other than the average sea level to be used. Syntatically, the subject is more narrow than the object as it expects the value in meters, whereas the object allows text to be added to explicit the convention used.

column3: Accuracy of the elevation value in meters.
For organisms collected beneath the surface of a water body. Measured in meters below the surface of water.
column12: The vertical distance below local surface. For sediment or soil samples depth is measured from sediment or soil surface, respectively. Depth can be reported as an interval for subsurface samples
column24: Both terms have the same meaning, although the object has some ambiguity, allowing for a reference point other than the average sea level to be used. Syntatically, the subject is more narrow than the object as it expects the value in meters, whereas the object allows text to be added to explicit the convention used.

column3: The accuracy of the depth value in meters.
A category or description of the habitat.
column12: Report the entity or entities which are in the sample or specimen s local vicinity and which you believe have significant causal influences on your sample or specimen. We recommend using EnvO terms which are of smaller spatial grain than your entry for env_broad_scale. Terms, such as anatomical sites, from other OBO Library ontologies which interoperate with EnvO (e.g. UBERON) are accepted in this field. EnvO documentation about how to use the field: https://github.com/EnvironmentOntology/envo/wiki/Using-ENVO-with-MIxS

column3: The names of, references to, or descriptions of the methods or protocols used to capture the associated specimen.
column12: The method employed for collecting the sample
column24: The object can include the names, references or descriptions of the methods used to collect a sample; similarly, the subject too with an expected structured pattern.

column3: Nucleotide sequence as submitted.
Count of IUPAC non-degenerate nucleotide symbols.
Accession number assigned to a sequence upon its deposition in one of the databases affiliated with the International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration, 'www.insdc.org'.
Source of funding for data generation, curation, and publication.
Short code associated with the sequenced marker(gene).
column12: Targeted gene or locus name for marker gene studies

column3: A comma separated list of forward primer code and sequence pairs, with each code and sequence separated by a colon.
column12: PCR primers that were used to amplify the sequence of the targeted gene, locus or subfragment. This field should contain all the primers used for a single PCR reaction if multiple forward or reverse primers are present in a single PCR reaction. The primer sequence should be reported in uppercase letters
column24: The subject expects only the sequence for the forward primer (narrower), while the object expects both the forward and reverse primer (broader). Syntatically, the subject expects a single nucleotide sequence per field, whereas the object defines a structured regular expression requiring both forward and reverse primer sequences to be provided within a single field, each prefixed by an identifier (e.g. ‘FWD:’ and ‘REV:’) and separated by a delimiter.

column3: A comma separated list of reverse primer code and sequence pairs, with each code and sequence separated by a colon.
column12: PCR primers that were used to amplify the sequence of the targeted gene, locus or subfragment. This field should contain all the primers used for a single PCR reaction if multiple forward or reverse primers are present in a single PCR reaction. The primer sequence should be reported in uppercase letters
column24: The subject expects only the sequence for the forward primer (narrower), while the object expects both the forward and reverse primer (broader). Syntatically, the subject expects a single nucleotide sequence per field, whereas the object defines a structured regular expression requiring both forward and reverse primer sequences to be provided within a single field, each prefixed by an identifier (e.g. ‘FWD:’ and ‘REV:’) and separated by a delimiter.

column3: Full name of the institution where sequencing was performed or the database the sequence was retrieved from.
Date the sequence was uploaded.
A comma separated list of Projects and Datasets on BOLD that this record is associated with.
Inferred RESOLVE defined biome based on the collection coordinates.
column12: Report the major environmental system the sample or specimen came from. The system(s) identified should have a coarse spatial grain, to provide the general environmental context of where the sampling was done (e.g. in the desert or a rainforest). We recommend using subclasses of EnvO s biome class: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ENVO_00000428. EnvO documentation about how to use the field: https://github.com/EnvironmentOntology/envo/wiki/Using-ENVO-with-MIxS
column24: The subject expect the biome as inferred through the collection coordinates, whereas teh object expects the broad-scale environmental context using subclasses of EnvO s biome class: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ENVO_00000428.

column3: Inferred RESOLVE defined ecoregion based on the collection coordinates.
inferred RESOLVE defined realm based on collection coordinates.
Institution with sovereignty over the physical specimen.
column12: The name of the institution where the sample or DNA extract is held or "sample not available" if the sample was used in its entirety for analysis or otherwise not retained
column24: The subject expects the name of the institution with sovereignty over the specimen, whereas the object expects the name of the institution storing the specimen. As such, we expect these to be exact matches semantically. However, syntactically, if the sample is not available, the object expects string 'sample not available', wheras the subject details no requirement.

column3: The date when the record was first created in the database in GMT (UTC+0).
The date when the record was last modified in the database in GMT (UTC+0).
